title: Galatians 6
subtitle: Bearing Burdens, Sowing and Reaping, the New Creation

heb.paragraph: Two different Greek words for ‘burden’ appear in vv. 2 and 5. In v. 2, barē (plural of baros) denotes heavy, crushing loads requiring communal help. In v. 5, phortion refers to a personal load or responsibility. Christians help one another with overwhelming burdens (v. 2) while each takes responsibility for their own proper duties (v. 5).

The agricultural metaphor of vv. 7–9: sowing to the flesh produces corruption (phthora), while sowing to the Spirit produces eternal life (zōē aiōnios). The present participle ho speirōn indicates a habitual pattern, not an isolated act.

refs.note: Paul’s reference to being ‘under Christ’s law’ parallels the ‘law of Christ’ in Gal 6:2.
The sowing-reaping principle applied to financial generosity parallels Gal 6:7–9.
Jesus’ teaching on removing the plank from one’s own eye provides background for Gal 6:1.

notes.note: Restoration is the church’s duty when a believer stumbles. The ones who restore must be ‘spiritual’ and must act ‘gently,’ aware of their own vulnerability. The ‘law of Christ’ is the command to love as Christ loved.
Paul warns against self-deception. Each person should test their own actions. Mutual burden-bearing does not eliminate personal responsibility.

The sowing-reaping principle is inviolable: ‘God cannot be mocked.’ Sowing to the flesh reaps corruption; sowing to the Spirit reaps eternal life. The exhortation to ‘not become weary in doing good’ addresses the persistence required.

Calvin reads the instruction as a corrective to both harshness and indifference. The spiritual person restores with gentleness reflecting the Spirit’s own character. The ‘law of Christ’ is the law of love that Christ both taught and embodied.
Calvin warns against presuming that grace nullifies moral consequences. Grace empowers believers to sow to the Spirit. The harvest is certain, though the timing is God’s.

The phrase ton nomon tou Christou is an ironic contrast with nomos as used for the Mosaic Torah throughout the letter. Paul replaces the Torah’s external regulation with Christ’s pattern of self-giving love.
The noun phortion is distinct from barē (v. 2): it refers to a soldier’s pack or personal responsibility.

Moo reads the instructions as the outworking of the Spirit-led life of 5:16–26. The ‘law of Christ’ is not a new legal code but the principle of self-sacrificial love exemplified in Christ. The sowing-reaping principle introduces an eschatological dimension: the Spirit-led life is the way that leads to eternal life.

Bruce notes that the ‘law of Christ’ is a deliberate counterpoint to the Mosaic law. The sowing-reaping metaphor draws on the wisdom tradition (Prov 11:18; Hos 8:7; 10:12) and applies it to the central issue: flesh vs. Spirit lead to two radically different destinies.

hist.context: Paul moves from the Spirit’s fruit to its practical applications in community life. The chapter opens with instructions for gentle restoration of a believer caught in sin (v. 1), followed by the command to ‘carry each other’s burdens’ (v. 2)—identified as the fulfilment of ‘the law of Christ’ (ton nomon tou Christou). This striking phrase, in a letter arguing against the Mosaic law, indicates Paul envisions a new ‘law’—the pattern of Christ’s self-giving love. The sowing-reaping principle (vv. 7–9) provides eschatological motivation. The exhortation to ‘do good to all people, especially to those who belong to the family of believers’ (v. 10) defines the scope of Christian responsibility.

sections.header: Verses 11–18 — "The Cross and the New Creation"

heb.paragraph: The letter’s climactic theological category. It transcends the circumcision/uncircumcision debate entirely: what matters is the new creative act of God that transforms believers into a new order of existence. The language echoes Isa 65:17 and signals that the Christ-event has inaugurated an eschatological reality rendering old categories obsolete.

Paul’s closing statement—‘I bear on my body the marks of Jesus’ (v. 17)—refers to physical scars from apostolic sufferings. The term was used for brands on slaves indicating ownership. Paul’s scars mark him as Christ’s slave—a status superseding and relativising the mark of circumcision.

refs.note: Paul’s declaration ‘if anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come’ directly parallels Gal 6:15.
The prophetic vision of new heavens and earth provides the OT background for Paul’s concept of kainē ktisis.
Paul’s redefinition of circumcision resonates with Gal 6:14–15, where the cross replaces circumcision as the marker of covenant identity.

notes.note: The agitators promote circumcision to make a ‘good impression outwardly’ and to avoid persecution. They do not even keep the law themselves—their religion is one of appearances, not substance.
Paul’s singular boast is in the cross. The new creation (v. 15) is the letter’s ultimate answer to the Judaizers: circumcision is irrelevant because a new order of existence has dawned.

The ‘Israel of God’ is best understood as the whole company of believers who walk by the rule of the new creation. The stigmata are Paul’s credentials, not circumcision. The closing benediction returns to grace, the letter’s governing theme.

Calvin finds here the summary of the entire Christian life. To boast in the cross is to renounce every other ground of confidence. The new creation is the divine reality that replaces all human categories. Calvin declares both circumcision and uncircumcision irrelevant. Only God’s new creative act matters.

Calvin identifies ‘the Israel of God’ with the church composed of believing Jews and Gentiles. He reads the kai as epexegetical: the church is the true Israel because it is the community of faith, the heirs of Abraham’s promise (3:29).
The phrase pēlikois grammasin is a dative of instrument. The shift to Paul’s own handwriting authenticates the letter (cf. 2 Thess 3:17; 1 Cor 16:21).
The phrase kainē ktisis lacks the article, suggesting a qualitative emphasis: a new kind of existence, not a specific entity.

Moo reads the postscript as a recapitulation in miniature: agitators’ motives exposed, the cross set against circumcision, the new creation declared the only thing that matters. Moo argues that ‘the Israel of God’ is one of the most debated phrases; he favours the epexegetical reading: the church is the Israel of God.

Bruce notes the autograph postscript was a common ancient convention. The exposure of the agitators’ motives is devastating. Bruce argues that ‘the Israel of God’ refers to the whole believing community, Jew and Gentile, who constitute the eschatological people of God.

hist.context: Paul takes the pen from his amanuensis and writes the closing in his own hand, using ‘large letters’ (v. 11). He exposes the agitators’ motives: they promote circumcision to avoid persecution for the cross (v. 12) and to boast in the Galatians’ flesh (v. 13). Paul’s own boast is in the cross alone (v. 14), through which the world has been crucified to him and he to the world. The climactic declaration is v. 15: ‘Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision means anything; what counts is the new creation.’ The letter closes with benediction on all who follow this rule, designated as ‘the Israel of God’ (v. 16).

rows.label: v.1
rows.text: Restore the fallen gently; watch yourself

rows.label: vv.2–5
rows.text: Bear burdens (the law of Christ); carry your own load

rows.label: vv.6–10
rows.text: Sowing and reaping; do good to all, especially the household of faith

rows.label: vv.11–13
rows.text: Postscript in large letters; the agitators’ motives exposed

rows.label: v.15
rows.text: Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision: the new creation

rows.label: v.16
rows.text: Benediction: peace and mercy on the Israel of God

rows.label: vv.17–18
rows.text: The stigmata of Jesus; final grace benediction

lit.note: Galatians 6 brings the letter full circle, from community ethics (vv. 1–10) to the cross-centred climax (vv. 11–18). The ‘law of Christ’ (v. 2) defines the normative pattern for the free community. The new creation (v. 15) is the letter’s ultimate theological category.

themes.note: The scores are balanced in this concluding chapter. Covenant, Faith, Sovereignty, and Mission all score highly as Paul summarises: the covenant community is defined by faith in the crucified Christ, governed by the law of love, and oriented toward the new creation.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Abraham
ppl.text: He reads the kai as epexegetical: the church is the true Israel because it is the community of faith, the heirs of Abraham’s promise (3:29)…

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 2)—identified as the fulfilment of ‘the law of Christ’ (ton nomon tou Christou)…

thread.text: Paul’s redefinition of circumcision resonates with Gal 6:14–15, where the cross replaces circumcision as the marker of covenant identity.

discourse.thesis: Bear one another's burdens in community, sow to the Spirit rather than the flesh, and boast only in the cross — in which the only thing that matters is the new creation.

nodes.text: Community responsibility for restoration: if anyone is caught in a transgression, you who are spiritual (πνευματικοί) restore him in a spirit of gentleness — watching yourself lest you too be tempted. Bear one another's burdens and so fulfill the law of Christ.

children.text: If anyone thinks himself something when he is nothing, he deceives himself. Each is to test his own work — then his boast will be in himself alone and not in his neighbor. For each will bear his own load. The apparent contradiction (v. 2 bear one another's, v. 5 bear your own) resolves: v. 2 = heavy moral burdens; v. 5 = personal accountability.

nodes.text: Share with the teacher in all good things. Do not be deceived — God is not mocked. Whatever one sows, he will reap. He who sows to his flesh will reap corruption; he who sows to the Spirit will reap eternal life. Do not grow weary in doing good — in due season we will reap if we do not give up.

children.text: Therefore (ἄρα οὖν) — as we have opportunity let us do good to everyone, especially to the household of faith. The eschatological harvest (v. 8) grounds the present ethical imperative.

nodes.marker: As many as desire to look good in the flesh (ὅσοι θέλουσιν εὐπροσωπῆσαι)
nodes.text: The agitators' true motive: to avoid persecution for the cross of Christ. They do not keep the law themselves — they want to boast in the Galatians' flesh. Paul boasts only in the cross, by which the world has been crucified to him and he to the world.

children.text: Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything — only the new creation (καινὴ κτίσις). Peace and mercy upon the Israel of God. The 'Israel of God' most likely refers to Jewish believers — Paul pronounces blessing on the full covenant community of faith.

nodes.text: Let no one cause trouble for Paul — he bears the marks of Jesus (τὰ στίγματα τοῦ Ἰησοῦ) on his body. The τὰ στίγματα are the scars of his apostolic suffering — the visible evidence that he does not avoid the offense of the cross. The grace benediction closes the letter with the same gospel word with which it opened (1:3).

discourse.note: Galatians 6 brings the letter's ethics to ground level: restoring the fallen, supporting teachers, sowing to the Spirit. The postscript (vv. 11-18), written in Paul's own hand, returns to the letter's opening polemic — exposing the agitators' motives as social/political (avoiding persecution) rather than theological. The letter ends on the cross and the new creation — the two realities that have made circumcision irrelevant.
